content:
{
  "title": "Calibration-by-Contrast: Enhancing LLM Confidence via Dynamic Comparison Prompts",
  "problem": "Large language models often produce poorly calibrated confidence estimates, leading to unreliable uncertainty quantification in their outputs. Existing prompting strategies either ignore or inadequately address this issue.",
  "motivation": "Accurate confidence estimation is crucial for deploying LLMs in high-stakes domains such as law and medicine, as well as for safe AI alignment. Recent work shows that LLMs are prone to overconfidence, and that their token probabilities do not always correlate with true answer correctness, especially on out-of-distribution or adversarial data.",
  "method": "We propose a prompting method that elicits uncertainty by presenting the LLM with contrastive candidate answers—one correct, one or more plausible distractors—and explicitly asks the model to quantify its confidence as a probability distribution over choices, together with a natural language explanation of its certainty. To further calibrate, we introduce meta-prompts that ask the model to assess how similar the current question is to those it 'feels' competent about, drawing on explicit analogical self-assessment. This dynamic, contrastive self-reflection prompt is designed to align LLM confidence scores with empirical accuracy.",
  "experiment_plan": "We will evaluate on MMLU, TruthfulQA, and a subset of MedQA, comparing our method against vanilla prompting, chain-of-thought rationale prompting, and existing calibration approaches like temperature scaling. Metrics include expected calibration error (ECE), Brier score, and selective accuracy under abstention. We will analyze performance across in-distribution, out-of-distribution, and adversarially-perturbed samples."
}
